entry.title: Clarification on Standard Processing of Multiple DELFOR IDocs
entry.content:
The sender system (Infineon) and the receiver system (Bosch) are both SAP systems.

Infineon sends Supplier Confirmations for the same Scheduling Agreement in two separate DELFOR IDocs (one containing Firm schedule lines and another containing Forecast schedule lines) in DELFOR D.97A/D.04A format.

We would like to understand the standard SAP behavior for processing these multiple inbound DELFOR IDocs. Specifically, how should SAP process Supplier Confirmations received in separate IDocs for the same Scheduling Agreement.

Expectation:

 

Is there any standard SAP functionality to merge or aggregate multiple inbound DELFOR IDocs into a single Supplier Confirmation before updating the Scheduling Agreement (EKES)? If yes, please provide the standard program, function module, class, or processing logic responsible for this scenario.

 

If no such standard functionality exists, please advise the recommended SAP standard approach for processing Supplier Confirmations received in multiple DELFOR IDocs for the same Scheduling Agreement.

entry.title: Title: [S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition] Automatic Payments (F0770): how to post the payment directly W
entry.content:
Title: [S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition] Automatic Payments (F0770): how to post the payment directly WITHOUT creating a payment order

Environment
- SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Release: <fill in, e.g. 2602>)
- App: Manage Automatic Payments – Fiori ID F0770

Current behavior
When I execute a payment run in Manage Automatic Payments (F0770), the supplier open items are NOT cleared at execution time. Instead they seem to be reserved as a payment order.
As a result, when I try to clear the same items manually in Post Outgoing Payments (F1612), the action is disabled and I get:
"Item(s) 1 are part of a payment order and cannot be selected for clearing"
(This matches KBA 3503165.)
So my current payment method appears to be running in "payment order" mode.

What I want to achieve
I want the payment run to DIRECTLY post the payment document at execution time and immediately clear the supplier open item (posting the offset to the bank clearing / bank sub account) — i.e. I do NOT want a payment order to be created.

What I have checked so far (for reference only – not sure this is correct)
I suspect the "Payment order only" flag at the country-level payment method settings is involved (possibly SSCUI 101972 – Set Up Payment Methods for Each Country/Region for Payment Transactions), but I am not sure whether this flag is editable / the correct lever in Public Edition.

My question
Could someone provide the concrete, step-by-step configuration procedure to achieve DIRECT posting (no payment order) in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, WITH the specific SSCUI IDs and Fiori App IDs for each step?
Specifically:
1. Exactly which setting needs to be changed, in which SSCUI (please include the SSCUI ID)?
2. What follow-on settings must be maintained (e.g. document type for payment in posting details)?
3. Any impact I should be aware of on Electronic Bank Statement clearing (since payment-order-number based matching would no longer apply)?
4. How to handle open items that are already blocked by an existing payment order?

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: Error while changing depreciation start date in asset...
entry.content:
Dear All!

When you change the depreciation start a recalculation of the asset values is being performed for all open fiscal years in asset accounting (last closed fiscal year can be seen in table T093B field ABGJA. The error you receive is usually cause by the fact that asset fiscal year is open and the posting periods are close in FI-GL transaction OB52. 

You should first close asset accounting and than the posting periods in order to avoid inconsistencies.

Please read SAP Note 3335047 Closing FI-GL before FI-AA (SAP S/4HANA only) ir

619969 Closing FI-GL before FI-AA

Best regards

Werner 

SAP Support

entry.title: Re: Duplicate GST Validation Triggered for Unregistered V...
entry.content: IN3 is specifically for GST Registration Numbers. If a vendor is not GST‑registered, the recommended approach is to leave IN3 blank. Entering “NA” is interpreted as an actual tax number, so the system enforces uniqueness. Having said that, it is not recommended to maintain IN3 with “NA” for unregistered vendors. The standard practice is to leave IN3 blank unless a valid GSTIN exists, and if you need to track unregistered status, use a separate tax category or BP attribute.

entry.title: Re: Change field selection in "Create Customer Project"
entry.content:
Hi @yKim,

Our UI colleague confirmed that the Cost Center field is rendered as a sap.m.Select control, which is a standard HTML-style dropdown list. It has no search or filter capability.

What you observed with English characters is not a search function. It is standard Select keyboard navigation: pressing a key on your keyboard moves the highlight to the first item in the list that begins with that character. For example, pressing "C" jumps to "Csltg Unit A (JP)". This is a browser-level behavior on any Select control, not an SAP search feature.

In the long term, submitting an Improvement Request via the SAP Customer Influence portal is the right channel to formally request SAP address the search limitation in this field.

Regards,

Sreevidya

entry.title: Re: Unable to retrieve the price list region-wise or mate...
entry.content: In S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the standard way to provide price lists is via CDS views and APIs (API_PRICING_CONDITION_SRV), not via legacy reports. For user‑facing analysis, use the Manage Prices – Sales app.

entry.title: Lessons from a Phase 2 SAP SF Rollout:-Governance, migration control, and partner accountability
entry.content:
About This Piece

I had the opportunity to work as the functional and technical Lead consultant on this engagement — a Phase 2 Employee Central and Time Off rollout that extended an already-live SAP SuccessFactors instance to two new countries in South-East Asia. My part in it covered configuration build, migration planning, UAT coordination, Production deployment along with working closely with the client's IT Governance & HR team and an existing AMS partner. It was a good learning experience, and the points below come straight from that project, not from a textbook.

Phase 2 Is a Different Animal

When you build SuccessFactors from scratch, there is always some room to adjust as you go along. Phase 2 does not give you that luxury. The instance is already live, real people are using it in other countries, and a change to a shared object — a Corporate Data Model, a global Business Rule, a Position structure, leave object etc— touches every region on the system, not just the ones you are rolling out to.

A few things make Phase 2 that much more demanding:

  • The system is already running Core HR ESS, MSS process including payroll and leave transactions for live users elsewhere, so mistakes have immediate consequences.
  • Shared objects belong to everyone on the instance, so even a small, well-intentioned change can ripple outward.
  • The stakeholder circle is wider too — IT Governance, AMS, HR teams from multiple countries, and the implementation team all need to know exactly where their role begins and ends.

Four Things Worth Getting Right

1. Lock Governance Early, and Keep It Locked

On this engagement, a bundle-only migration rule was introduced two-thirds of the way through build, without an impact assessment or a timeline review. It cost us roughly two weeks of rework, and a fair bit of back-and-forth to get everyone aligned again. It taught us, quite plainly, that governance decided upfront is worth far more than governance revisited midway.

What we would recommend:

Lock governance, RACI, and the migration approach during Discovery, before a single configuration object is built. If the approach must change later, let it go through a proper impact assessment and sign-off first.

2. Not All Configuration Objects Are the Same

Standard, country-specific configuration moves through a bundle smoothly — you build it, transport it, validate it, done. Shared global objects need a gentler hand. On this project, a Corporate Data Model change, Position object change, leave object change meant for the new countries had to be manually checked against the live configuration already used by three other regions before we could touch it.

What we would recommend:

Use a hybrid migration model. Let standard, country-scoped configuration e.g. country specific data model changes, country specific custom objects, benefits, Leaves etc go through Transport Management as usual, and handle shared global objects manually, with a documented comparison against Production each time.

3. Draw Clear Lines Between Partners

Most Phase 2 programs bring together at least four parties — the Implementation Partner, the HR team, the AMS partner, and the client's IT Governance team. On our project, the AMS partner was set up for support-level work, but ended up in an implementation-grade role e.g. all UAT migration to be done with implementation consultants on call. Our consultants stayed engaged during migration process explaining  migration tracker , bundles, migration fixes, defect fixes, to help out, time that could otherwise have gone into test scripts, user guides, and proper knowledge transfer.

What we would recommend:

Agree on a simple competency matrix upfront. Implementation owns build, migration, and UAT fixes; AMS can shadow from UAT if they are ready for it, and takes over formally from Hypercare onwards.

4. Treat Data Migration as Implementation Work, Not an Admin Task

Data migration deserves the same seriousness as configuration build. On this engagement, ownership of the Production upload shifted twice — first over data privacy concerns, then over readiness —during UAT it was done by AMS partner under the guidance of Implementation consultant. During production migration, process got changed, it was initially given to HR before it settled with the implementation Consultants.

What we would recommend:

Fix data ownership, access, and privacy approvals at least 60 days before Go-Live, and make sure whoever runs the Production upload has already done at least one full mock load in QAS. Please note that Core HR data migration is a significant activity that involves uploading data across more than 20 templates. Therefore, the technical competency of the nominated resource should be thoroughly assessed before deciding whether to assign the activity to the HR team or the AMS partner.

 

A Quick Word on UAT

UAT on a shared instance genuinely needs more care, since QAS is shared too. But care should not mean everything grinds to a halt. On our project, one small, three-field configuration fix took two full days to move through the standard approval cycle. That experience is what led the team to introduce a same-day Emergency Fix Protocol for low-risk defects, with AMS reviewing the change rather than gatekeeping it. It made a real difference to morale during a demanding phase of the project.

What We Learned: Summary

Area

What Happened

What Helped

Governance

Migration approach changed mid-build, with no impact review.

Lock governance and RACI during Discovery; any later change goes through a formal impact assessment.

Migration Strategy

A bundle-only rule was applied even to shared global objects.

Use a hybrid model — standard config via Transport Management, shared objects handled manually.

Partner Roles

AMS was scoped for support but stepped into an implementation-grade role.

Agree a competency matrix upfront; AMS formally takes over from Hypercare.

Data Ownership

Upload ownership shifted twice, quite late in the project.

Lock ownership 60+ days out; require a full QAS mock load before Production. Assess technical competency of the nominated role for Data upload.

UAT Agility

Every fix, big or small, went through the full approval cycle.

Introduce an Emergency Fix Protocol for low-risk defects.

A Final Thought

Phase 2 programs bring their own kind of pressure, but they also bring some of the best team moments — the late evenings sorted out together, the small wins that nobody outside the project ever hears about. Most of what we have shared here is entirely preventable, simply by getting a few things right before execution begins rather than fixing them in the middle of it.

These lessons come from genuinely being in the room when things got a little difficult, and figuring our way through them as a team. If you are heading into a Phase 2 SuccessFactors rollout and want to think through governance, migration, or your partner model, we would be happy to have that conversation. And if you have your own lessons from a similar journey, do share them — that is exactly the kind of thing the SAP community grows stronger from.

Thank you!

Mohammad Saddam Aquib

entry.title: Re: PurchaseRequests API - RequriedDate Field with Typo (...
entry.content:
Hi,

 

unfortunately, this is not the only case, there are also database fields with typing errors in names and this has become something "standard". I do not believe this will be changed as it would affect any previously developed functionalities which utilize the "incorrect" fields. 

I suggest first using GET for any object as a rule of thumb, and then using that structure to create your own POST.

 

BR,

Matija

entry.title: MDK Action of type ODataService.CallFunction, can have Nested JSON Array as an input parameter
entry.content:
Hi Team,

We have created an action of type "Action.Type.ODataService.CallFunction" , it has several input parameters among which one of the parameter products is nested JSON Array as shown below.

PRODUCTS :[ {
product_code : 101279812,
product_desc : 'PC321 Lubricants and oils',
Batch_code : [  {
batch_code : 1
batch_product : 'Engine Oil'
qty : 5,
uom : 'ltr'
},
{batch_code : 2,
batch_product: 'Brake Lubricants 1ltr'
qty : 2,
uom : 'ltr'
}],
quantity : 3,
UOM : '',
Unit_price:''
}]

on executing, MDK Framework is rejecting the request stating below error

Error running action propagating error Action.Type.ODataService.CallFunction.2: Error: [-10440] Unrecognized datatype of parameter "products" for action "createDelivery******". : Error: [-10440] Unrecognized datatype of parameter "products" for action "createDelivery******".\n at ODataServiceProvider.toJSError (file:///data/data/com.sap.mobileservices.client/files/app/vendor.js:2:1737430)\n at file:///data/data/com.sap.mobileservices.client/files/app/vendor.js:2:1750507\n at async ExecuteAction (file:///data/data/com.sap.mobileservices.client/files/definitions/bundle.1872.js:11902:20)\n at async Rule.CreateDeliveryDocket [as ruleFunction] (file:///data/data/com.sap.mobileservices.client/files/definitions/bundle.1872.js:10571:26)

 

The request is not even reaching the back end odata services, as the frame work is throwing above errors, so can we pass a json array or nested json array as an input parameter ?

 Regards

Govardan Raj

entry.title: Re: Duplicate GST Validation Triggered for Unregistered V...
entry.content: Hi Lakshmipathi, Thank you for your clarification. We tested the recommended approach of leaving the **IN3 (GST Registration Number)** field blank for GST-unregistered vendors. However, we encountered another issue. When we select **Tax Number Category = IN3** and leave the tax number blank, the system displays the following validation error: > **"Enter a tax number for the tax category."** As a result, we are unable to save the Business Partner without entering a value in the GST Number field. Could you please advise on the standard SAP approach for this scenario? * Is there any configuration that allows the **IN3** tax number to remain blank for GST-unregistered vendors? * If **IN3** should not be maintained for unregistered vendors, what is the recommended way to indicate that a vendor is GST-unregistered? * Should the **IN3** tax category itself be omitted for unregistered vendors, or is there another standard tax category or Business Partner attribute that should be used instead? We would appreciate your guidance on the SAP standard process for handling GST-unregistered vendors in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Thank you.

entry.title: Power BI Refresh Failure When S/4HANA Is Unavailable Despite Persisted Data in SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
We have the following data flow:

SAP S/4HANA (On-Premise) → SAP Datasphere (CDS Remote Table) → Persisted Data → Power BI

The data from the CDS Remote Table has been successfully persisted in SAP Datasphere. However, when the SAP S/4HANA source system is unavailable, the Power BI dataset refresh fails.

Expected Behavior:
Since the data is already persisted in SAP Datasphere, Power BI should be able to refresh successfully by reading the persisted data without requiring connectivity to the SAP S/4HANA source system.

Actual Behavior:
When SAP S/4HANA is unavailable, the Power BI refresh fails, indicating that there is still a dependency on the source system even though the data has been persisted.

Request:
Please help us understand:

* Why does the Power BI refresh still depend on SAP S/4HANA after the data has been persisted in SAP Datasphere?
* Is this expected behavior for persisted data based on CDS Remote Tables?
* Are there any additional configurations or best practices required to ensure that Power BI reads only from the persisted data without requiring connectivity to the source system?

Please advise on the root cause and the

Any first-hand experience or pointers to official documentation would be really appreciated. Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: Sales Order to AR Invoice query
entry.content:
SELECT DISTINCT

T0.DocEntry,

CASE
WHEN T2.DocDate IS NOT NULL
THEN CAST(YEAR(T2.DocDate) AS VARCHAR(4)) + '-' +
RIGHT(CAST(YEAR(T2.DocDate)+1 AS VARCHAR(4)),2)
ELSE ''
END AS [Invoice FY],

T4.SeriesName,
T0.DocNum AS [SO No],
T0.DocDate AS [SO Date],
T0.CardCode as [Customer Code],
T0.CardName as [Customer Name],

CASE T0.U_Type
WHEN 1 THEN 'Sales'
WHEN 2 THEN 'Service'
WHEN 3 THEN 'Blank'
WHEN 4 THEN 'Adv-PO-Sales'
WHEN 5 THEN 'Adv-PO-Service'
WHEN 6 THEN 'Sales - Life Sciences'
WHEN 7 THEN 'Service - Life Sciences'
WHEN 8 THEN 'Sales - FD'
WHEN 9 THEN 'Service - FD'
WHEN 10 THEN 'Sales - MSF'
WHEN 11 THEN 'Service - MSF'
END AS'Type',

T0.U_Region as [Region],
(T0.DocTotal-T0.VatSum) as 'SO Total',

CASE
WHEN T1.LineStatus = 'O' AND T0.CANCELED = 'N'
THEN (SELECT Sum([LineTotal]) FROM RDR1 WHERE DocEntry = T0.DocEntry AND LineStatus='O') ELSE 0 END AS [Open SO Amount],

CASE
WHEN T1.LineStatus = 'C' AND T0.CANCELED = 'N'
THEN (SELECT Sum([LineTotal]) FROM RDR1 WHERE DocEntry = T0.DocEntry AND LineStatus='C') ELSE 0 END AS [Closed SO Amount],

CASE
WHEN T0.CANCELED = 'Y'
THEN (T0.DocTotal-T0.VatSum) ELSE 0 END AS [Cancelled SO Amount],

--CASE
-- WHEN T0.CANCELED = 'Y' THEN 'Cancelled'
-- WHEN T0.DocStatus = 'C' THEN 'Closed'
-- WHEN T0.DocStatus = 'O' THEN 'Open'
--ELSE 'Unknown'END AS [SO Status],

T2.DocNum AS [Invoice No],

--CASE
-- WHEN T2.CANCELED = 'N'
-- THEN Sum((T3.[Price]*T3.Quantity))
--END As 'Inv Total'

T2.DocDate AS [Invoice Date],
(T2.DocTotal-T2.VatSum) as 'INV Total'

--CASE
-- WHEN T2.CANCELED = 'Y' THEN 'Cancelled'
-- WHEN T2.DocStatus = 'C' THEN 'Closed'
-- WHEN T2.DocStatus = 'O' THEN 'Open'
--ELSE 'Unknown'END AS [Invoice Status]



FROM ORDR T0
INNER JOIN RDR1 T1 ON T0.DocEntry = T1.DocEntry
LEFT OUTER JOIN dln1 T3A ON T1.DocEntry = T3A.BaseEntry AND T1.LineNum = T3A.BaseLine AND T3A.ItemCode=T1.ItemCode
LEFT OUTER JOIN INV1 T3 ON T3."BaseEntry" = (case when T3."BaseType"='17' then T1.DocEntry when T3."BaseType"='15' then T3A.DocEntry else 0 end)
AND T3."BaseLine" = (case when T3."BaseType"='17' then T1.LineNum else T3A.LineNum end)
AND T3."ItemCode" = (case when T3."BaseType"='17' then T1.ItemCode else T3A.ItemCode end)
LEFT OUTER JOIN OINV T2 ON T3.DocEntry = T2.DocEntry
LEFT outer JOIN NNM1 T4 ON T0.Series = T4.Series
LEFT JOIN RDN1 T5 ON T5.BaseType = 15 AND T5.BaseEntry = T3A.DocEntry AND T5.BaseLine = T3A.LineNum

Where
--T0.DocDate BETWEEN [%0] AND [%1] and (T2.docDate IS NULL OR T2.DocDate <= [%1])
T0.DocEntry = '71380'
--and T5.DocEntry IS NULL
--and T0.CANCELED = 'Y'
--and (T2.CANCELED = 'N')

Group By T1.Price,
T1.Quantity,
T3.[Price],T3.Quantity,
T1.DiscPrcnt,
T0.DocNum,
T0.DocDate,
T2.DocNum,
T2.DocDate,
T0.CANCELED,
T2.CANCELED,
T0.DocStatus,
T2.DocStatus,
T0.CardCode,
T0.CardName,
T0.DocEntry,
T4.SeriesName,
T0.DocTotal,
T0.VatSum,
T1.LineTotal,
T1.LineStatus,
T2.DocTotal,
T2.VatSum


ORDER BY
T0.DocDate,
T0.DocNum,
T0.CardCode

 

 

Regards,

Aagam S.

entry.title: Can SAP API Management directly invoke SAP S/4HANA OData services without SAP Cloud Integration?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I have an architectural question regarding the use of SAP API Management and would appreciate your insights.

Scenario

I have a client application that sends data to an API Proxy in SAP API Management. Instead of forwarding the request to an SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) iFlow, I am considering calling an SAP S/4HANA OData service directly from the API Proxy.

Since the target OData service requires an X-CSRF token for POST operations, the API Proxy would need to perform the following steps:

  1. Receive the incoming request from the client.
  2. Fetch the X-CSRF token from the SAP S/4HANA OData service.
  3. Reuse the token (and cookies, if required) to execute the POST request.
  4. Return the response back to the client.

My Questions

  1. Is this architecture technically feasible using only SAP API Management, without involving SAP Cloud Integration (CPI)?
  2. Can SAP API Management reliably handle the complete X-CSRF token flow (token retrieval, cookie handling, and POST request)?
  3. Is this approach recommended for production environments, or are there any technical limitations or best practices that should be considered?
  4. What would be the major drawbacks compared to using SAP Cloud Integration, particularly regarding:
    • Error handling
    • Retry mechanisms
    • Monitoring and logging
    • Security
    • Scalability
    • Maintainability

Why I'm Asking

The motivation behind this approach is to optimize SAP Cloud Integration message consumption and potentially reduce licensing costs for simple pass-through scenarios. If API Management can securely and reliably handle such integrations, it could eliminate the need for an iFlow in certain use cases.

Has anyone implemented a similar architecture in a production environment? If so, I would appreciate hearing about your experience and whether you would recommend this approach.

Thank you in advance for your guidance!

entry.title: S/4 HANA BRF+: In Sales Order system is triggering the output with Channel = Print instead of Email?
entry.content:
Dear All,

In S/4HANA BRF+, I’m facing a challenge: in change mode of Sales Order, the system is triggering the output with Channel = Print instead of Email for output type: ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE

Scenario:

  1. Sales Order Creation (VA01), Sales Org: UK01, Order Type: ZOR, Shipper ST maintained in PA40, Requested Delivery Date: 07.07.2026, Quantity: 1, Schedule Line Date: 07.07.2026, Confirmed Qty: 1, Save → Output Type ORDER_CONFIRMATION triggered successfully.
  2. Sales Order Change (VA02), Requested Delivery Date: 31.07.2026, Quantity: 2, Schedule Line Date: 31.07.2026, Confirmed Qty: 2, Save → Output Type ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE triggered with Channel = Print instead of EMAIL

Requirement: Whenever there is a change in delivery date or quantity, the system should:

  • Trigger ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE output automatically with channel EMAIL.

We have maintained the below rules:

show rules for - SALES DOCUMENT

Determination Set

output type - (Document Cat. =C, Sales Org.=UK01, Sales Document type <>BV exclude =SO01, =CCIS(Consignment Issue), Output Type VA01 - Sales Order created with Requested Delivery date - 07.07.2026, Qty-1, Schedule line date - 07.07.2026, confirmed qty = 1 save, Dispatch Time - 1 (Immediate)

Receiver - Output type =ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE, Role - ST, Exclusive Indicator -(False)

Channel - Output type =ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE, Sales Org - UK01, Role = ST, Recipient (Blank), Channel - EMAIL, Exclusive Indicator -(False)

Printer Setting - Output type =ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE, Role (Blank). Printer Queue - ZPDF, Number of Copies - 1

Email Setting - Output type =ORDER_CONFIRMATION_CHANGE,Role =ST, Sales Org =UK01, Sender Email - UK_GS@gcs, Email Template - ZZ_SD_SLS_Default_Email_SO

entry.title: Business Data Architecture: Transformations - Making Architectural Boundaries Enforceable
entry.content:
Understanding architecture is one thing. Enforcing it is another.

You understand the concepts - modeling layers organize data flow, data product types structure reuse, platforms own domains. But when a developer asks: "Can I add Revenue Growth % to my Definition layer data product?" - how do you answer?

Without explicit rules, the answer is judgment: "Probably not, that feels like semantic layer logic." With 10 developers making judgment calls, you get 10 different interpretations. Architectural boundaries dissolve.

Transformations make boundaries enforceable. They classify data transformations and specify which layers and data product types each can be used in. The question "Can I add Revenue Growth % to Definition Layer?" gets a concrete answer: "No. That's KPI Calculation - only allowed in Semantic Layer."

This isn't about one developer building one data product. It's about 50 developers across 5 platforms making consistent decisions over 3 years. The architecture stays intact because the rules are explicit, not implicit.

This post defines the transformation framework. It establishes which transformations are allowed in which scope (layer + data product type), provides the catalog structure for organizing them, and includes the process for defining new transformations. Let's start with what transformations actually are in Business Data Architecture.

Transformations

Transformations are the mechanism that makes the framework enforceable. They're logical patterns (not platform-specific code) that define what kinds of change can happen to data - categorized into two types based on their scope.

Two Transformation Categories

1. Business Transformations - Layer-specific

  • These transformations implement business logic - changing data meaning, structure, or interpretation based on business rules. Each belongs to specific layers based on its purpose. Examples: Currency Conversion, KPI Calculation, Join Across Sources, Data Type Normalization.

2. Operational Transformations - Allowed everywhere

  • These transformations support observability, audit trails, and operational metadata without changing business meaning. They're allowed in any layer because they track operational state, not business logic. Examples: Add Load Timestamp, Add Lineage Metadata, Add Process ID, Add Data Quality Metrics.

Transformation Anatomy

Whether operational or business, every transformation is defined using the same six-part structure. This consistency ensures that anyone in your organization can look at a transformation definition and immediately understand what it does, where it belongs, and what rules apply.

Here's what every transformation contains:

This structure makes transformations complete and actionable - you know what it does, where you can use it, and what rules you must follow.

What Transformations Are NOT

You now understand the anatomy - six elements that define any transformation. But before we show you what a populated transformation catalog looks like, there's a critical distinction to establish: transformations are logical patterns that describe change, not the code that executes it. Here's what that means:

Transformations are logical patterns that describe WHAT change happens to data. They are NOT:

  • Artifacts - Not SQL views, dbt models, Python scripts, Databricks notebooks, or any platform-specific code
  • Implementation details - Not HOW the change is executed, just WHAT change occurs
  • Platform-specific - The same transformation (e.g., "Currency Conversion") can be implemented differently on Snowflake, SAP Datasphere, or Databricks

Example: "Currency Conversion" is a transformation - a logical pattern. It might be implemented as a Snowflake SQL view, a dbt model, or a Python notebook. The transformation defines WHAT (convert currency values), WHERE (which layers), and WHAT LIMITS apply (constraints). The artifact is HOW it's actually executed.

The relationship between transformations and artifacts is covered in our next post on artifact roles - which artifacts can execute which transformations.

You now understand what transformations are: logical patterns with six elements, categorized as operational or business, distinct from the code that implements them. But understanding the concept isn't enough - everyone needs to reference the same definitions.

The Transformation Catalog

How do you ensure 50 developers across multiple platforms all use the same transformation definitions?

A central catalog - your repository of defined transformations that serves as the single source of truth. Without it, each team invents their own "Data Masking" or "Currency Conversion" - same names, different implementations, inconsistent results.

The catalog could be a wiki, a repository, or a metadata system. What matters is that it's:

  • Centralized - One place, not scattered across teams
  • Accessible - All developers can find and reference it
  • Governed - Changes go through a formal process
  • Versioned - Track when transformations are added or deprecated

Catalog Structure

Here's what a populated catalog looks like, organized by layer and category. This is NOT exhaustive - these are examples showing structure.

 

Key observations:

  • Ingestion layer: Not shown because it uses only operational transformations - preserving source data without business logic
  • Define/Apply pattern: Reference data products define mappings once (e.g., Define Source Metric), and other data products apply them in their specific layers (e.g., Apply Source Metric Definition in Definition layer)
  • Multi-layer transformations: Some business transformations appear in multiple layers with different purposes. For example, Access Control appears in both Semantic layer (row-level security for analytical data products) and Sharing layer (consumption-level security for all exposed data)

This catalog gives you a shared vocabulary and consistent patterns across your organization. But what happens when you need a transformation that doesn't exist yet?

Adding New Transformations

Adding a new transformation requires formal definition and communication to ensure consistent reuse across teams.

This five-step process ensures every transformation in your organization follows the same structure, gets reviewed for consistency, and becomes discoverable to all teams. It's the difference between 50 developers each inventing their own "Currency Conversion" and 50 developers reusing one well-defined transformation.

The transformation framework gives you the governance mechanism to keep architecture intact at scale. Transformations define what's allowed where, the catalog organizes them, and the process ensures they're defined consistently.

You now have the complete transformation framework: two categories (operational and business), six-element anatomy, a catalog structure to organize them, and a process to add new ones. This framework makes architectural boundaries enforceable - turning abstract concepts like "layer separation" into concrete rules developers can follow.

Equally important: transformations give you a common language across platforms. When a Snowflake developer and a Datasphere developer both implement "Currency Conversion," they're following the same logical definition - same purpose, same constraints, different code. This prevents business logic from fragmenting into platform-specific interpretations. One architectural definition, multiple platform implementations, consistent behavior.

What's next

Transformations define what logic belongs where (layer + data product type). But how do you ensure a SQL view executes only the transformations it's allowed to? Or that a stored procedure doesn't violate layer boundaries?

This is where Artifact Roles come in. You know what artifacts are - SQL views, stored procedures, graphical views. But not all artifacts can execute all transformations. The next post introduces roles that constrain which transformations an artifact can execute, ensuring your platform implementations respect the architectural boundaries you just defined.

This blog post is part of the Business Data Architecture for Modern Analytics Landscapes series.

⬅️  Previous Post | Next Post  ➡️

entry.title: Re: Mass change order quantity in Purchase order SAP Publ...
entry.content:
Dear @Moon3,

Your observation is expected. Apart from the Mass Changes to Purchase Orders app, there does not seem to be another standard SAP app in Public Cloud for mass updating different Order Quantities on already existing Purchase Order items. I would also not position Migration Cockpit or Excel upload as standard options for this requirement, as I have not found SAP documentation confirming their use for operational mass updates of existing PO item quantities.

From a technical perspective, SAP provides the released Purchase Order OData V4 API / API_PURCHASEORDER_2. SAP Help confirms that this API can be used to create, update or delete purchase orders, and the operation list includes Change purchase order schedule line quantity using PATCH. Therefore, this can be evaluated as a custom integration option, but not as a standard Fiori mass-change function or SAP standard workaround

This API option must be validated carefully. SAP KBA 3669206 confirms that PATCH works for standard PO type NB but custom PO types must be copied correctly from NB and unsupported item categories can result in operation is not enabled. The KBA also confirms that service item category D is not supported

So there is no standard SAP Fiori app or SAP-confirmed standard workaround to mass change PO item Order Quantity for existing purchase orders. If the requirement is for a SAP-delivered standard mass-change function, it should be raised through SAP Customer Influence.

More details below:

SAP Help – Mass Changes to Purchase Orders

SAP Help – Purchase Order (OData V4)

SAP Help – Operations for Purchase Orders

SAP KBA 3669206 – Unable to perform PATCH operation on custom purchase order type using API_PURCHASEORDER_2

SAP KBA 2963059 – How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: SAC date variable input as MM/DD/YYYY?
entry.content:
Hi @Preet_Singh ,

As far as I am concerned, there's no standard, SAP-supported setting that makes a date variable's manual free-text entry accept MM/DD/YYYY. This is a known, long-standing platform gap, not something your Datasphere layer design can fix.

entry.title: Re: Private Insight rights in SAC
entry.content:
Hi @Tuggi ,

Private Insight is a real, standalone permission. It's not meant to require Create on Private Files. Per SAP Help, Private Insight is documented as its own Object Type with only a Create checkbox, specifically designed so viewer-role users without Create rights on Public/Private Files can still save, share, and delete (but not rename/edit in the repository) their own insights.
So functionally, your expectation is correct Create on Private Files is a much broader grant (it also opens up Story creation) and shouldn't be the only path to this behavior.


If Private Insight genuinely isn't selectable in your role editor, this looks like a tenant-specific gap rather than expected behavior. One thing worth checking first: permission visibility in custom roles can be filtered by license type try building the role from a blank template instead of templating from Viewer, and see if it appears. If it still doesn't show up, I'd raise an incident referencing the "Private Insight" entry in the Permissions documentation, since the privilege is clearly meant to exist independently of Private Files.

entry.title: Re: Generate Warehouse Tasks

the sequence for creating warehouse task with document reference is a real problem inside EWM. I already were in touch with sap support to a similiar problem. My problem was not with report /SCWM/R_DLV_PDO_CREATE_WT, but also with  the sequence inside the /SCWM/TO_PREP_WHR_UI_INT if you are creating cross delivery warehouse task/orders.  Unfortunately the support couldn't fix the issue.

In your case are creating warehouse task for every single delivery with report /SCWM/R_DLV_PDO_CREATE_WT. Inside there is done a technical SORT by GUIDs. These Guids are not in sequence. They are random generated. As a conesquence there is no stable SORT.

You can create a support ticket, an maybe they change the behaivor of the report. If not and your business requirement is to create warehouse task for each delivery you could easily copy the report and change the Sort behaivor.

entry.title: Re: Parameters of Multi action not taking updated story f...
entry.content:
Hi @Shaifali_Jain ,
Not a bug, just a known limitation. Cascading Effect only updates what's shown in the Cost Object filter dropdown, not its actual stored selection. If that filter's real selection is still "All Members" at the top node, that's what the multi action parameter reads so it expands to every L2, ignoring the cascade.

SAP documents this under "Unsupported Cases for Linking Parameter Values": "The available members list includes the All Members selection... Deselecting the All checkbox in the list of selected members won't fix the problem."
Here are some workarounds you could try:

  1. Set the Cost Object filter's actual selection to an explicit member (not "All Members" at the top node).
  2. Skip the auto-link use a script to read the currently selected member after cascading and call setParameterValue() on the multi action manually before running it.

Option 2 is more robust long-term.

entry.title: Re: Need Centralized Maintenance of Date Input Controls A...
entry.content:
Hi @Suraj34 ,
SAP doesn't provide a single "update once, apply everywhere" mechanism for input controls across separate stories but in most cases like this, the better fix is to remove the need for manual maintenance entirely rather than centralize it.
1. Switch to Dynamic Date Range Filters
If the date selection is something like "last month" or "current quarter," use a Dynamic Date Range Filter instead of a static date pick. These recalculate automatically based on the system date, so nobody has to reset them on the 1st of the month. This alone usually eliminates the missed-report problem, since there's nothing left to forget.
2. Custom Current Date (CCD) if you need a fixed reference date, not "today"
If your use case genuinely needs a specific reference date (not just "today"), each story supports one Custom Current Date input control, which all dynamic date range filters within that story can link to. Set the CCD once and every linked filter in that story recalculates from it. Note this is per-story it doesn't sync across your other 19 reports.
3. True cross-report propagation of one date value
There's no native SAP feature for this. If you need it anyway, the practical options are:

  • Launch all reports from a central point (Digital Boardroom, collection, or launchpad) and pass the date via URL parameters (mParams) so it's set on open requires a shared entry point rather than opening each story individually.
  • If the reports share a model, drive the date restriction from a model-level variable rather than a story filter, so updating it once at the model updates every story built on that model.
  • In Optimized Story Experience, a script object using Application.setVariableValue() can set it programmatically, but only within the story it runs in it doesn't broadcast to other stories.

Bottom line: start with #1. It's very likely the actual root cause of your "reports get missed" problem, and it removes the maintenance task rather than centralizing it.

entry.title: Setting Up SAP Joule on S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: A Technical Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP’s generative AI copilot designed to enhance user productivity across SAP applications. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition, Joule empowers users to discover applications, access contextual business insights, and navigate business processes conversationally directly within the SAP Fiori experience.

This document provides a comprehensive, end-to-end technical guide for setting up SAP Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition. It is written from an administrator and platform engineering perspective, explaining not only how to configure each component, but also why it is required and how the overall integration functions.

Architecture Overview

 

At a high level, Joule runs on SAP BTP and is embedded into the SAP user experience using SAP Build Work Zone. Authentication and authorization are handled centrally through SAP Cloud Identity Services.

Prerequisites and Readiness Checklist

Before proceeding with the setup, ensure the following prerequisites are in place:

Authorizations

  • SAP BTP Global Account Administrator
  • SAP BTP Subaccount Administrator
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS & IPS) Administrator
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Administrator

Entitlements

  • SAP Joule (Digital Assistant Service – das-application)
  • SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition

Landscape Requirements

  • Supported for both 2-System Landscape (2SL) and 3-System Landscape (3SL)
  • SAP Cloud Connector is not required for Public Cloud systems

Technical Preconditions

  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant registered in BTP System Landscape
  • SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant available and accessible
  • Browser allows iFrames and third-party cookies

Preparation

SAP BTP Subaccount Strategy

A dedicated SAP BTP subaccount is typically used to host Joule and SAP Build Work Zone. From a design perspective, regional alignment with SAP Cloud Identity Services is recommended to minimize authentication latency and reduce cross-region dependencies.

The subaccount acts as the orchestration layer where:

  • Joule services are provisioned
  • Work Zone acts as the user entry point
  • Trust and identity integration converge

Identity Trust and Domain Alignment
Single Sign-On between SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP BTP, and SAP Build Work Zone is achieved through trust establishment with SAP Cloud Identity Services.

A critical design consideration here is domain alignment:

  • The IAS domain selected must align with the domain model of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant
  • Mismatched domains are a frequent root cause of repeated authentication prompts and intermittent Joule access issues

Trusted domain configuration on both BTP and IAS is essential for enabling secure cross-origin communication and embedded user experiences.

Identity Attribute Readiness
Joule and SAP Build Work Zone rely on a consistent identity model provided by IAS. Certain core user attributes must be available and consumable by downstream applications.

From an operational perspective:

  • Attribute completeness matters more than attribute volume
  • Missing verification-related attributes can result in silent authentication failures that are difficult to trace at runtime

Ensuring identity consistency early on significantly reduces troubleshooting effort later.

Joule Enablement Model
Guided Onboarding via Booster

SAP provides a Joule Booster that automates the majority of the technical enablement steps. This guided onboarding mechanism establishes the baseline configuration required for Joule to operate within the BTP subaccount.

Conceptually, the booster:

  • Validates entitlements and platform readiness
  • Establishes system formations
  • Creates the necessary connectivity artifacts
  • Activates SAP-delivered communication scenarios

 

Architecturally, this step should be treated as a managed onboarding process rather than a manual configuration exercise.

SAP Build Work Zone Integration

SAP Build Work Zone serves as the experience layer through which Joule is surfaced to end users. Key design elements include:

  • Subscription to the Work Zone service
  • Secure consumption of S/4HANA Cloud content
  • Centralized role-based navigation and access control.

Work Zone consumes S/4HANA Cloud content using SAP-delivered exposure mechanisms, ensuring that only authorized applications, catalogs, and groups are visible.

Content Exposure Model
Content exposure from SAP S/4HANA Cloud into SAP Build Work Zone is governed by standard communication scenarios delivered by SAP. These scenarios enable controlled sharing of Fiori content without custom integration.

From a best-practice perspective:

  • Content exposure should be validated, not modified
  • Role and catalog assignments remain the authoritative control mechanism

This approach preserves clean-core principles while enabling rich user experiences.

Identity Provisioning Architecture
Identity Provisioning Services (IPS) are used to synchronize users and role assignments into SAP Build Work Zone.

Rather than being treated as a one-time activity, identity provisioning should be viewed as:

  • A continuous synchronization mechanism
  • A dependency for consistent authorization behavior
  • A key operational component for Joule visibility

Operational validation of IPS jobs and logs is a critical checkpoint during the initial enablement.

Validation and Operational Readiness
Once the platform setup is complete, validation should focus on the end-to-end user experience rather than on individual configuration items.

Typical validation checkpoints include:

  • Successful single sign-on from SAP S/4HANA Cloud
  • Visibility of SAP Build Work Zone content
  • Availability of the Joule interaction entry point
  • Execution of basic conversational queries

A successful validation confirms that Joule is correctly embedded and operational.

 

Common Operational Observations
Based on real-world implementations, the following patterns are frequently observed:

  • Repeated login prompts usually indicate trust or domain misalignment
  • Missing Joule entry points are commonly linked to role assignment or identity synchronization gaps
  • Empty or incomplete content typically points to content exposure or IPS issues

Systematically addressing these areas resolves the majority of post-enablement issues.

Great to see your interest in Financial Accounting. If you're just getting started, I recommend beginning with the SAP Learning site, Financial Accounting there you can learn how to process, analyze, and automate intelligent accounting solutions. You find structured, role-based learning journeys designed specifically for beginners and aspiring consultants. SAP offers guided learning paths, hands-on practice opportunities, and certifications that help you build both conceptual knowledge and practical skills. 

A good learning sequence would be:

Wishing you success on your SAP learning journey—stay curious, stay persistent, and enjoy the process of turning knowledge into expertise! 

Kind regards
Margit

entry.title: Re: Reprocessing of Retried Messages in SAP CPI
entry.content:
Hi Venkat,

Please find below the available options for handling failed messages in SAP CPI based on the two scenarios:

Scenario 1: Sender System Messages Not Delivered to CPI

In this case, the message never reached CPI, so the retry must be handled on the sender side:

  • SFTP — The file remains in the source folder and will be re-polled on the next scheduled run
  • IDoc — Retry can be triggered from the SAP backend via SM58 / BD87
  • HTTP/SOAP — The calling system must resend the request
  • AS2 — The sender must resend after receiving a negative MDN from CPI

Note: These messages will not appear in the CPI monitor as they never entered the system.

Scenario 2: Messages Reached CPI but Failed During Processing

For messages that entered CPI but failed within the integration flow, the following options are available:

  • Monitor → Message Processing — View error details and manually retry the failed message
  • JMS Dead Letter Queue — Reprocess messages from the DLQ via Monitor → Manage Stores → JMS Resources
  • Data Store — Retrieve stored message payload and reprocess manually
  • Automatic Retry — Retry count and intervals can be configured within the adapter settings
  • Alert Rules — Alerts can be set up to notify the team via email upon failure

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model - Introducing Dynamic Packaging Type in Partitioning
entry.content:
Hello, SAP Community members! 

If you have worked with the Partitioning in SAP Profitability and Performance Management On-Premise, you already know how powerful it is. The ability to split a large dataset into ranges and run your processing functions package by package is, in many models, the difference between a job that completes in minutes and one that ties up your system for hours. The idea is elegant: define your ranges once at design time, and the engine does the rest. 

For SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model customers, this raises a natural question: does the Cloud Partitioning give me the same control I had On-Premise, including the ability to determine ranges dynamically at runtime rather than locking them in at design time? With the introduction of Dynamic Packaging Type, the answer is yes. 

The Dynamic Packaging Type in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model follows the same principle as its On-Premise counterpart: ranges are no longer pre-defined as inner nodes but are determined at runtime from the input data of the function where partitioning is applied. For background on the On-Premise partitioning and packaging setup, see the SAP Help Portal documentation.

Meet the Dynamic Packaging Type in Partitioning

The Dynamic Packaging Type is found in the Partitioning within the General Entities. This is a new packaging option that brings a fundamentally different approach to how processing operations are split across data ranges at runtime. Until now, only Static packaging was available in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model; you define your ranges as inner nodes at design time, and those ranges are fixed for every run. 

One key structural difference worth knowing upfront: with Dynamic Packaging, you no longer define range nodes inside the Partitioning at all. Any range nodes previously defined inside a Static Packaging are removed when you switch to Dynamic. The ranges are built fresh at runtime, derived from the input function entries of the function where dynamic partitioning is applied.

Configuring the Partitioning: Packaging Type

Expand the Partitioning in your model. The Properties panel now includes the new field placed directly after Run Mode. 

Packaging Type: Determining How Ranges Are Defined 

This is the central new field introduced by this enhancement. 

Packaging Type (dropdown, mandatory): Determines whether ranges are defined at design time or derived at runtime. 

The dropdown offers two values: 

  • Static 
  • Dynamic 

Static preserves the existing behavior — ranges are defined as inner nodes inside the Partitioning at design time, exactly as before. 

Dynamic is the new Packaging Type introduced by this enhancement. Unlike Static, where ranges are locked in at design time, Dynamic determines partition ranges at runtime — derived automatically from the input function entries of the function where dynamic partitioning is applied. Note that switching an existing Partitioning from Static to Dynamic removes any range nodes previously defined inside it. 

The default value for Packaging Type is Static. Existing Partitioning built without this field are treated as Static automatically. No migration of existing configurations is required. 

Packaging Field: Defining the Field for Dynamic Range Creation 

Another mandatory companion to Packaging Type, present for both Static and Dynamic configurations. 

Packaging Field (dropdown, mandatory): Defines which field values are used to create the partition ranges at runtime.

Note: The field previously labeled Field in the Partitioning has been renamed to Packaging Field. This rename applies to all Partitioning, both Static and Dynamic configurations. 

Before You Begin

Before configuring a Dynamic Packaging Type, keep the following constraints in mind: 

  • Range Levels and Destinations are not supported for Dynamic Packaging Type. These features are only available with Static packaging. 
  • For Dynamic Packaging Type, the following Run Mode values are not displayed in the dropdown when Dynamic is selected: PPBP, PPDP, SPXP, SUXP. 
  • For a given Packaging Field, you can define multiple Partitioning as long as each has a different Run Mode or Packaging Type setting.

How It Works

To help illustrate, here is a complete end-to-end walkthrough of configuring and running a Partitioning with Dynamic Packaging Type. 

  1. Open your model in the Modeling environment and locate the Partitioning you want to configure (or create a new one from the Palette). 
  2. Open the Properties panel. Locate the Packaging Type field, placed directly after Run Mode. 
  3. Set Packaging Type to Dynamic.  
  4. Set Packaging Field to the field whose values will drive range creation at runtime. Confirm that this field exists as an input to the function where dynamic partitioning will be applied. 
    Note that PPBP, PPDP, SPXP, and SUXP Run Mode values will not be displayed in the Run Mode dropdown when Dynamic Packaging Type is selected.
  5. Save the Partitioning configuration. 
  6. Execute your process. At runtime, the system reads the distinct values of the Packaging Field from the input function entries and constructs the partition ranges dynamically before processing begins. 
  7. Check the Application Monitor to confirm the run completed and to see how many package instances were created. In our example, if the input function contains entries for four customers, the engine creates four partition ranges at runtime and processes each as a separate package instance. 

This is the complete end-to-end flow: the Packaging Type field drives whether ranges come from design-time nodes or runtime data, the Packaging Field identifies the dimension that structures those runtime ranges, and the engine handles range creation automatically before execution begins. See SAP Help related to Partitioning and Range Setup for further details. 

Wrapping Up

The Dynamic Packaging Type removes a configuration step that, for many models, was also a maintenance burden: keeping design-time range nodes synchronized with the actual data in your system. Whether you work with a cost center hierarchy that changes frequently and you want packaging to always reflect the current structure, a profit center landscape that varies by version and requires a different set of ranges for each scenario, or a model where the right partition boundaries simply cannot be known until the data arrives at runtime, Dynamic Packaging Type gives you a clean, supported way to let the data define its own partitions. 

We are eager to hear from you. Which datasets in your models are you thinking of migrating from Static to Dynamic packaging? Have you encountered situations where design-time range maintenance was a limiting factor in your partitioning strategy? Share your thoughts in the comments below and stay tuned for further enhancements to the Partitioning in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model. 

As always, feel free to reach out via the SAP Profitability and Performance Management Community if you have questions. 

Related Resources 

  • Partitioning and Range Setup - SAP Help Portal (SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model) 
  • Different Run Modes - SAP Help Portal (SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model) 
  • Partitioning and Packaging Setup - SAP Profitability and Performance Management On-Premise

entry.title: Building Vector Search Applications with SAP HANA Cloud and LangChain.js
entry.content:
Imagine your customer types “something comfortable for working from home” into your shop’s search bar. There’s no “comfortable” column in your product table. No “work from home” tag either. And yet your store somehow needs to surface the ergonomic chair, the noise-cancelling headphones, and the standing desk.

That gap between what people say and what your database stores is exactly where vector search lives. With @SAP/hana-langchain, your SAP HANA Cloud database doesn’t just store your data. It understands it.

The full walkthrough, with every line of code, every output, and every config option, lives on GitHub: blogs/blog-post.md. The sections below focus on the ideas worth remembering, paired with the bits of code and output that bring them to life.

What it is

@SAP/hana-langchain is the official open-source bridge between SAP HANA Cloud’s Vector Engine and LangChain.js. It turns your enterprise database into a first-class AI retrieval layer for RAG, semantic search, recommendations, and intelligent Q&A, all running where your data already lives.

Two lines and you have a vector store:

npm install @langchain/core @langchain/classic langchain
npm install @sap/hana-langchain

Searching by intent, not by keywords

Let’s walk through the real story. A customer is setting up their home office. They open your store and search:

“comfortable work from home setup”

Your catalogue has hundreds of products. Keyword search? Zero relevant hits. With HanaDB? Watch what happens.

import { HanaDB, HanaInternalEmbeddings } from "@sap/hana-langchain";

// Embeddings happen INSIDE HANA. No external API. No data leaving your DB.
const embeddings = new HanaInternalEmbeddings({
  internalEmbeddingModelId: "SAP_NEB.20240715",
});

const store = new HanaDB(embeddings, {
  connection: client,
  tableName: "PRODUCT_CATALOG",
});
await store.initialize();

const results = await store.similaritySearch(
  "comfortable work from home setup",
  2,
);

The output:

[
  Document {
    pageContent: 'Ergonomic office chair with lumbar support and adjustable armrests',
    metadata: { category: 'furniture', price: 449, in_stock: true }
  },
  Document {
    pageContent: 'Professional espresso machine with built-in grinder',
    metadata: { category: 'appliances', price: 899.99, in_stock: false }
  }
]

The chair makes obvious sense. The espresso machine? Also surprisingly on-brand for “working from home.” The model picked up on intent that no SQL LIKE ever could.

Cosine vs. Euclidean: pick your distance

So how does similaritySearch actually decide which products are “closest” to the query? Each document and the query are turned into vectors, and then HanaDB applies a distance metric to measure how far apart they are. The library supports two: cosine (the default) and Euclidean. You pick which one you want with a single config option when you create the store.

The choice matters because the two metrics score the same set of candidates differently. Take the query “high quality audio headphones” and run it under both strategies. Same data, same embedding model, different scoring philosophy:

COSINE Similarity Results (higher = more similar)
[0.7219] Wireless noise-canceling headphones with premium sound quality
[0.5506] Professional studio microphone for podcasting and streaming
[0.5206] Bluetooth speaker with deep bass and 20-hour battery

EUCLIDEAN Distance Results (lower = more similar)
[0.7086] Wireless noise-canceling headphones with premium sound quality
[0.9048] Professional studio microphone for podcasting and streaming
[0.9251] Bluetooth speaker with deep bass and 20-hour battery

Both metrics pick the same winner here, but notice the gap between first and second place: cosine spreads the candidates by relative similarity, Euclidean by raw distance in vector space. As a rule of thumb, cosine is the right default for text, because it only cares about the direction the vectors point in, not how long they are. Euclidean is a better fit when the actual position of the vectors carries meaning, for example when your embeddings come from numerical features rather than text. If you’re not sure which to pick, leave it on cosine.

Adding diversity when you need it: MMR

Sometimes “most similar” is exactly what the user wants. A shopper who searches for “wireless headphones” expects a list of wireless headphones, and similarity search delivers that perfectly well.

But there are spots in the same product journey where pure similarity hurts the experience. The classic case is the “customers also viewed” strip on a product detail page. A shopper looking at a premium open-back studio headphone doesn’t need to see four more premium over-ear headphones below it. They’d be much better served by a strip that also shows them a different form factor, a different price tier, or a noise-cancelling alternative.

That’s the job of Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR). Instead of returning the top-k most similar documents, MMR picks results that are both relevant and different from each other. You retrieve a larger candidate pool; MMR walks it, picking the next item that maximises relevance while penalising redundancy with what’s already been chosen. One lambda knob tunes the balance between relevance and diversity.

Same vector store, same query, two different methods. First, plain similarity search:

// Plain similarity search: top-4 candidates ordered by closeness alone
const similar = await vectorStore.similaritySearch(
  "premium wireless over-ear headphones",
  4,
);
similaritySearch top 4:
  1. AudioMax Pro 5     (over-ear-wireless, $349.99)
  2. AuroraSound Pro    (over-ear-wireless, $379.99)
  3. AudioMax Buds 5    (true-wireless,     $299.99)
  4. QuietShield Ultra  (over-ear-wireless, $429.99)

Three of the four results are premium over-ear wireless headphones, almost interchangeable for the shopper. Not a useful “customers also viewed” strip.

Now the same query, through MMR:

// MMR: pick 4 from a wider candidate pool while penalising redundancy.
// lambda = 0.5 balances relevance and diversity evenly.
const recommendations = await vectorStore.maxMarginalRelevanceSearch(
  "premium wireless over-ear headphones",
  { k: 4, fetchK: 12, lambda: 0.5 },
);
maxMarginalRelevanceSearch top 4:
  1. AudioMax Pro 5     (over-ear-wireless, $349.99)
  2. FlexFit Sport      (neckband,           $89.99)
  3. AuroraSound Pro    (over-ear-wireless, $379.99)
  4. TuneCore 510       (on-ear-wireless,    $49.99)

The top match is still the most relevant premium over-ear pair, but the next three deliberately span different form factors and price tiers. The shopper now sees an actual mix to compare, all from one method swap on the same vector store and the same product catalogue.

Use similaritySearch when the goal is the closest match. Reach for maxMarginalRelevanceSearch when the goal is coverage: recommendation strips, RAG context that spans multiple sub-topics, related-articles modules, or anywhere a single dominant theme would crowd out everything else.

Filtering that speaks JavaScript

Semantic search is great. Semantic search plus structured filters is unbeatable. HanaDB’s filter syntax will feel instantly familiar to anyone who’s ever written a MongoDB query:

// In-stock electronics under $500
const filter = {
  $and: [
    { in_stock: true },
    { category: "electronics" },
    { price: { $lt: 500 } },
  ],
};

const results = await store.similaritySearch("best audio quality", 10, filter);

And the result is exactly what the filter promised: only in-stock electronics, all under $500, ranked by audio quality:

[
  { pageContent: 'Premium wireless headphones with spatial audio...',  price: 349.99, in_stock: true },
  { pageContent: 'Budget wireless earbuds with decent sound...',       price:  49.99, in_stock: true },
  { pageContent: 'Bluetooth over-ear headphones with active noise...', price: 249.99, in_stock: true },
  { pageContent: 'Wireless noise-canceling headphones with 30-hour...',price: 299.99, in_stock: true }
]

You get the full operator suite ($lt, $lte, $gte, $between, $in, $nin, $like, $contains, $and, $or) all compiled down to native HANA SQL. No JavaScript-side filtering. No surprises at scale.

Letting the LLM write its own filters

Here’s where it gets fun. What if a user types “Show me AudioMax electronics under $400 with good ratings”? You could build a custom intent parser. Or you could let the LLM do it for free, with the HanaTranslator:

const retriever = SelfQueryRetriever.fromLLM({
  llm,
  vectorStore: store,
  documentContents: "Product description for an e-commerce catalog",
  attributeInfo,                              // describe your metadata
  structuredQueryTranslator: new HanaTranslator(),
});

await retriever.invoke("Show me AudioMax electronics under $400 with good ratings");

Behind the scenes, the LLM emits:

{
  $and: [
    { brand: "AudioMax" },
    { category: "electronics" },
    { price:  { $lt:  400 } },
    { rating: { $gte: 4 } }
  ]
}

Natural language in. Structured filter out. Vector search on top. Zero glue code.

There’s more in the box

What you’ve seen so far is the foundation. The library ships with three more capabilities, each big enough to deserve its own deep-dive:

  • Cross-encoding reranking with HanaReranker. Re-score your top candidates for surgical-precision results, the secret sauce behind great RAG and customer-facing search.
  • Knowledge graphs & SPARQL with HanaRdfGraph. Query RDF data, build natural-language Q&A over relationships, and combine graph reasoning with vector search.
  • Performance at scale with Map Merge, HNSW indexes, and specific metadata columns. Roughly 9× faster bulk inserts, sub-second search at million-document scale, and faster filtered queries.

Stay tuned for more updates and deep dives into each of these capabilities!

Get the code: SAP/langchainjs-integration-for-sap-hana-cloud  |  Read the full guide: blog-post.md

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2023 Integration with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management (SFM)
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am working on integrating SAP S/4HANA On-Premise 2023 with SAP Sustainability Footprint Management (SFM) and would appreciate your guidance on the recommended integration approach.

I have the following questions:

Q1. Is SAP CPI mandatory for integrating SAP S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise with SAP SFM?

Q2. Is the sap-sfm-integration-accelerator package mandatory for integrating SAP S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise with SAP SFM, or is it optional?

Q3. The SAP SFM 'Manage Source Systems' app lists SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud Edition as a supported source type for CDS-based replication. Does this same replication and extension framework (Manage Replications + Configure Extensions for Replications) also apply to On-Premise 2023, or does On-Premise require a different integration path?

Q4. How can transactional data be imported into SAP SFM? If I want to route a standard CDS view to a custom CDS view during the replication process, where can this mapping or redirection be configured?

Q5. What is the BADI for implementing customer-specific rules for MD and TD in SAP S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise? In SAP SFM, the "Configure Extensions for Replications" app only exposes a CDS View option for extensibility, and no BADI option is available. For scenarios where a CDS view is not sufficient for MD/TD, is there a supported BADI or another enhancement framework for implementing customer-specific MD and TD logic in this integration with SAP S/4HANA 2023 On-Premise? If so, where is it exposed and how can it be implemented?

Thank you for taking the time to read our questions. We greatly appreciate any guidance, suggestions, or experiences you can share.

Regards,
Shiva

entry.title: Re: Inbound Delivery Exceeds PO Quantity Error in SAP S/4...
entry.content:
Dear @laxmiprasad_rkhasnis,

The error M7 022 – PU Ordered quantity exceeded is caused by the quantity in the inbound delivery / goods receipt process exceeding the quantity still allowed for the referenced purchase order item

I reproduced the scenario in Public Cloud as follows:

  • PO quantity: 5 PC

  • Inbound Delivery quantity changed to 6 PC and Putaway to 6 PC

 

  • During the inbound delivery change, the system displayed ME160 Notified quantity exceeds quantity still to be notified 5 PC
  • The inbound delivery could still be saved
  • During stock placement / putaway and posting of goods receipt the system stopped the process with:

M7 022 – PU Ordered quantity exceeded by 1 PC

This confirms that the inbound delivery can be saved with a higher quantity but the system blocks the final goods movement / GR posting when the quantity exceeds the allowed PO quantity

The behavior is standard. During goods receipt the system checks the receipt quantity against the PO quantity and the allowed overdelivery tolerance. If the delivered quantity exceeds the allowed tolerance, the system issues an error

To resolve the issue there are three possible options:

1. Reduce the inbound delivery / putaway / GR quantity
Use this option if the business should only receive the quantity ordered on the PO. In this case the quantity should be reduced to the remaining open PO quantity

2. Increase the PO quantity
Use this option if the ordered quantity itself is incorrect and the PO should really be changed from, for example, 5 PC to 6 PC

3. Maintain overdelivery tolerance on the PO item
Use this option if the PO quantity should remain unchanged but the business allows receipt of a higher quantity

In the PO item in the Delivery section/tab, maintain either:

  • Overdelivery Tolerance Limit, for example 20% if PO quantity is 5 PC and receipt quantity is 6 PC or
  • Unlimited Overdelivery Allowed, if the business wants to allow overdelivery without a percentage limit

For the reproduced example, PO = 5 PC and GR = 6 PC the overdelivery is 1 PC, which equals 20%. Therefore setting Overdelivery Tolerance Limit = 20.0 allowed the GR to be posted successfully. The Underdelivery Tolerance Limit is not relevant for this error because it controls receiving less than the ordered quantity, not more

If the process is warehouse-managed and the error remains in the warehouse queue the corrected document should be reprocessed from the relevant warehouse processing / message queue after the PO quantity or overdelivery tolerance has been corrected

More details below:

SAP Learning – Applying Tolerances and the Delivery Complete Indicator

SAP KBA 3538235 – Error Message M7 022 at stuck queue for inbound deliveries – SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: S/4 HANA BRF+: In Sales Order system is triggering th...
entry.content:
Why it defaults to Print is because

Channel determination precedence
In BRF+, if multiple channel rules are valid, the system often picks the first matching one. If your Printer Setting rule (with blank role) is evaluated before the Email Setting rule, the system will assign Print.

Role mismatch
You have defined Email settings for Role = ST (Ship‑to). If the change scenario does not pass Role = ST correctly (e.g., Ship‑to not determined or missing in the change context), the Email rule won’t match, and the fallback “Printer” rule is applied.

Exclusive indicator
Since you set Exclusive = False, multiple rules can be valid. That increases the chance that the Print rule is picked first.

Form Template condition
You have tied the form template to Channel = Email. If the system does not find a matching form template for the change case, it reverts to the default Print channel.


So the next step is

  • Run a BRF+ trace for the VA02 change scenario. This will show exactly which rules are being evaluated and why the Print channel wins. From there, you can:
    • Tighten the conditions on the Printer rule,
    • Or make the Email rule exclusive and prioritized.

entry.title: Academic Resources for Teaching End-to-End Business Processes
entry.content:
Academic Resources for Teaching End-to-End Business Processes

Dear Lecturers,

Join us on July 28 2026 from 10:00–11:00 AM ET | 16:00–17:00 CEST for a live show-and-tell session showcasing three free resources designed to support teaching end-to-end business processes with SAP S/4HANA Cloud. 

  • Online Course – A free 32-hour course available on SAP Learning Hub, student edition
  • Practice System – Access to a preconfigured live SAP S/4HANA Cloud environment
  • Academic Lessons – Ready-to-use teaching materials for the classroom

Got a question? Submit it before the session!

Have something specific you'd like us to address? Comment on this forum post - we'll use your input to tailor the session to your interests.

Interested? Learn more and register here:  Webinar End-to-End Business Processes

We look forward to welcoming you!

Best regards,
SAP University Alliances Program

 

 

Not yet a member of the SAP Community? Here is how to get started.

To leave a comment, you need to be a registered member of the SAP Community with an SAP Universal ID. You may already have one if you have used SAP Learning Hub, student edition before. Click on the avatar in the top right corner to check if you can log in. 

If you do not have an SAP Universal ID yet:

  1. Start the registration process at SAP Universal ID registration.
  2. Add a username in the "Username" field. This name will be displayed in the SAP Community. If you leave it empty, a name will be filled in automatically.
  3. Check your email for an activation link and complete the registration.

If you already have an SAP Universal ID:

  1. Sign in to the SAP Universal ID account manager.
  2. Select Edit.
  3. Enter or update your display name in the "Username" field and click Save.

Important: Please use your real name as your username. Avoid using your email address, company or university name, or random characters.

Once you have your SAP Universal ID, sign in to the SAP Community:

  1. Go to the SAP Community.
  2. Click the avatar icon at the top right.
  3. Enter your login name or email address and password.
  4. Accept the terms and conditions.
  5. You are ready to go!

Best regards,
SAP University Alliances program

entry.title: Get Ahead of What's Next: Join the Early Release Series
entry.content:
Be First to Know What’s Next

Curious about the innovations shaping the future of supply chain? The Early Release Series gives you a front row seat. It's a biannual event where SAP product experts walk you through what's coming next in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, before everyone else.

What You'll Get Out of It

Across several days, you can join expert-led demos, gain insider insights, and take part in real-time Q&A. That means early access to new features and direct answers from the people building them. It's a practical way to see live demos, ask your questions, and plan ahead with confidence.

Sessions for the SCM Community

Here are four sessions we think you'll find valuable. Each one is a detailed, expert-led deep dive.

Artificial Intelligence — July 13, 2026, 12:30pm – 2:00pm CET

Take a broader look at where AI is heading. This session covers the Autonomous Enterprise, Joule for Business, workflow automation, and Autonomous SCM, including production, planning, and inventory agents that connect directly to your supply chain work.

Supply Chain Management — July 16, 2:00pm – 2:45pm CET

Discover the latest innovations in Supply Chain Management and see how they can support your day-to-day operations.

Manufacturing — July 14, 4:00pm – 4:45pm CET

Explore the newest manufacturing innovations, from engineering and planning to shop-floor execution. You'll see how AI-driven manufacturing agents help streamline decisions, automate routine tasks, and strengthen operational resilience. Expect practical demos, insider tips, and a clear view of what's next, so you can accelerate value from day one.

Quality Management — July 16, 4:30pm – 5:15pm CET

Get up to speed on the latest innovations in Quality Management and what they mean for your processes.

 

How to Sign Up

An active SAP Learning Hub license is required to register for the live sessions and to watch the recordings afterward.

If you're a customer:

You'll need access to at least the SAP Enterprise Support edition. Here's how to sign up:

  1. Go to the sign-up page and select Sign up.
  2. Authenticate with your S-user ID and password.
  3. The system checks your eligibility, creates your SAP Learning Hub user, and populates your learning catalogue. This step can take up to 2 hours.
  4. You'll get a confirmation e-mail once registration is complete.

If you're a partner:

With a valid SAP Universal ID and access to the Empowering SAP Partners site, you can join the live sessions and recordings directly.

Don't Miss Out

These sessions won't wait, and neither should you. Secure your spot to gain early insights, watch live demos, and stay ahead with the newest SAP updates.

Register today and be first to know what's next: learning.sap.com
